parentQuestion: During childbirth, cervical stretch triggers oxytocin release, which intensifies contractions, causing more cervical stretch and more oxytocin release. This cycle accelerates until delivery occurs. A medical student asks, 'Why doesn't this kill the mother by continuing indefinitely?' What's the answer?

scaffolds.q: POSITIVE feedback AMPLIFIES the original stimulus.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Unlike negative feedback which opposes change, positive feedback accelerates it.

scaffolds.q: Childbirth uses positive feedback: stretch → oxytocin → stronger contractions.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Each contraction leads to more oxytocin and even stronger contractions.

scaffolds.q: Positive feedback loops are self-accelerating - they keep getting stronger.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! That's why they need an external termination event.

scaffolds.q: If positive feedback continued forever, it would destroy the system.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Which is why positive feedback loops MUST have an endpoint.

scaffolds.q: In childbirth, the endpoint is DELIVERY - the baby is born.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Once the baby is delivered, cervical stretch stops and the loop ends.

scaffolds.q: Positive feedback requires an EXTERNAL termination event.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The loop doesn't stop itself - something external must end it.

scaffolds.q: Delivery removes the stimulus (cervical stretch), terminating the feedback loop.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! No more stretch = no more oxytocin trigger = contractions stop.

scaffolds.q: Positive feedback loops REQUIRE external termination events.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! They can't stop themselves - they need an external endpoint.

scaffolds.q: The answer: positive feedback requires an external termination event (delivery).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not pain stopping it, not homeostasis overriding, not desensitization.
